column1: THIS IS UNDER CONSTRUCTION, PLEASE DISREGARD Item

column5: A stone, usually a smooth river stone, that sheds bright daylight in a 60' radius
A padlock that cannot be picked, and only opens when the bearer of a specific ring, keyed to this particualr lock, touches it.
A paper lantern that sheds about 5 candles of light magically.

column3: paper lantern, sand, and a bioluminescent lifeform

This spell enchants a small paper lantern to glow with a soft light of the same color as the sand used in the casting. The lantern produces ambiant light equal to 5 candles without producing any heat. Anyone who touches or holds the lantern may light, dim, brighten (to it's maximum), or extinguish the light. Sheds 30' of soft light.
a cube of rock or metal

Wishing he could keep his drinks cold on hot summer days, the notoriously recluvie wizard Poorvan created this spell to chill his drink. A 1x1x1 inch cube of rock is enchanted to always be chilled. These stones only conduct heat/cold when immursed in fluids. One stone will keep one cup at a pleasent temperature. These stones will never be cold enough to cause harm to any living creature.
a bit of wool, and a pound of oil or fat which must be massaged into the item

When an article of clothing is enchanted with this spell it becomes unnaturally hydrophobic. A shirt enchanted thusly could be submerged in a bucket and come out dry. While the intent was to create a wonerful raincoat, an unforseen side affect is that enchanted items are very difficult to clean. Most other fluids (alcohol, oil, blood, etc.) are repelled. Acids are not.
